quotes.text: For all men, as for my children, I desire … complete happiness and welfare.
quotes.source: Rock Inscriptions of Asoka (Separate edicts, no. 1)

quotes.text: He lets his mind pervade one quarter of the world with thoughts of love, and so the second, and so the third, and so the fourth. And thus the whole wide world, above, below, around, and everywhere, he continues to pervade with heart of love, far-reaching, grown great, and beyond measure.
Whatsoever living beings there are, feeble or strong, … small or large, seen or not seen, … may all creatures be happy-minded.
A wise man never resents with passion the abuse of the foolish.
Yield not (one moment) to the angry impulse.
Overcome anger by love.
Like as the lotus … is untarnished by the water, so is Nirvana by any evil dispositions.
quotes.source: Questions of King Milinda (Book 4, ch. 8, sec. 66)

quotes.text: In agreement with all men, and hurting nobody, … he, as far as possible, does good to all.
Reverently practising the four gracious acts—Benevolence, charity, humanity, love; Doing all for the good of men, and that they in turn may benefit others.
They also, resigning the deathless bliss within their reach, worked the welfare of mankind in various lands. What man is there who would be remiss in doing good to mankind?
When first I undertook to obtain wisdom, then also I took on me to defend (the weak). All living things of whatsoever sort call forth my compassion and pity.
After having refrained from hurting all beings, both those that are strong and those that tremble in the world.

Because the dove fears the hawk, with fluttering pennons she comes to seek my protection. Though she cannot speak with her mouth, yet through fear her eyes are moist. Now, therefore, I will extend (to this poor creature) my own protection and defence.
An unselfish virtuous man.
The outward form not affecting religion.
Neither is it right to judge men’s character by outward appearances.
The body may wear the ascetic’s garb, the heart be immersed in worldly thoughts: … the body may wear a worldly guise, the heart mount high to things celestial.
FULL of truth and compassion and mercy and long-suffering.
UPRIGHTNESS is his delight.
MAKING ... virtue always his first aim.
AN example for all the earth.
WHAT he hears here he repeats not there, to raise a quarrel against the people here.
HE injures none by his conversation.
AIMING to curb the tongue, ... aiming to benefit the world.
INTENT upon benefiting thy fellow-creatures.
WALK in the path of duty, do good to your brethren, and work no evil towards them.
HEALTH is the greatest of gifts, contentment the best of riches.
IF thou be born in the poor man's hovel, yet have wisdom, then wilt thou be like the lotus-flower growing out of the mire.
THE rich who is not contented endures the pain of poverty.
THE words of Buddha, even when stern, yet ... as full of pity as the words of a father to his children.
quotes.source: Questions of King Milinda (Book 4, ch. 3, sec. 18)

quotes.text: OVERCOMING all enemies by the force (of his love).
HOW great his pity and his love toward those who opposed his claims, neither rejoicing in their defeat, nor yet exulting in his own success!
THE Buddha has mercy even on the meanest thing.
HE that ... would wait upon me, let him wait on the sick.
THE Buddha, O king, magnifies not the offering of gifts to himself, but rather to whomsoever ... is deserving.
quotes.source: Questions of King Milinda (Book 4, ch. 6, sec. 14)

quotes.text: IF you desire to honour Buddha, follow the example of his patience and long-suffering.
RADIANT with heavenly pity, lost in care For those he knew not, save as fellow-lives.
quotes.source: Sir Edwin Arnold (Light of Asia, bk. 5)

quotes.text: WHO that hears of him, but yearns with love?
